Home
PFAS in My Community Federal Regulations State Legislation
State-by-State Tracker

States step up as federal PFAS protections erode

As the Trump EPA rolls back drinking water limits for four of the six PFAS compounds the Biden administration regulated, state legislatures have accelerated their own restrictions — on drinking water, firefighting foam, food packaging, textiles, and cosmetics.

Data: Safer States PFAS Bill Tracker

189PFAS bills adopted into law
197PFAS bills under consideration in 2025–26
0PFAS bills under consideration in 2026
0issue areas
0states

Alaska

Year Status Bill Issue Notes
2026 Introduced S.B.219
Introduced
Requires annual testing of drinking water from all public water systems for PFAS.
 Water Requires annual testing of drinking water from all public water systems for PFAS.
2026 Introduced H.B.235
Introduced
Requires annual testing of drinking water from all public water systems for PFAS. Businesses involved in the production, use, or disposal of PFAS may face potential liability costs.
 Water Requires annual testing of drinking water from all public water systems for PFAS. Businesses involved in the production, use, or disposal of PFAS may face potential liability costs.
2024 Adopted S.B.67
Adopted
Prohibits the use of a firefighting substance that contains a perfluoroalkyl substance or polyfluoroalkyl substance. Establishes a firefighting substances disposal reimbursement program.
 Firefighting Foam Prohibits the use of a firefighting substance that contains a perfluoroalkyl substance or polyfluoroalkyl substance. Establishes a firefighting substances disposal reimbursement program.

California

21 adopted · 2 introduced · 23 total
Year Status Bill Issue Notes
2026 Introduced S.B.1313
Introduced
Authorizes the California State Water Resources Control Board to fund projects that address PFAS in drinking water and source water.
 Water Authorizes the California State Water Resources Control Board to fund projects that address PFAS in drinking water and source water.
2026 Introduced A.B.1603
Introduced
Prohibits the use, manufacturing, sale, and distribution of pesticides containing PFAS.
 Other Prohibits the use, manufacturing, sale, and distribution of pesticides containing PFAS.
2025 Adopted A.B.1181
Adopted
Requires the state to set a timeline and safety standard to phase out PFAS in turnout gear starting in 2028.
 Firefighter Gear Requires the state to set a timeline and safety standard to phase out PFAS in turnout gear starting in 2028.
2024 Adopted S.B.1266
Adopted
Restricts bisphenols in juvenile products, effective January 1, 2026.
 Children's Products Restricts bisphenols in juvenile products, effective January 1, 2026.
2024 Adopted A.B.2515
Adopted
Prohibits any person from manufacturing, distributing, selling, or offering for sale in the state any menstrual products that contain regulated PFAS.
 Personal Care Products Prohibits any person from manufacturing, distributing, selling, or offering for sale in the state any menstrual products that contain regulated PFAS.
2023 Adopted S.B.101
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Provides $90 million for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Other Appropriations bill. Provides $90 million for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
2022 Adopted SB 502
Adopted
Reforms aspects of California’s Safer Consumer Products program. Makes California’s program more efficient by granting data call-in authority to the program, streamlining processes (including giving the program the authority to regulate on the basis of alternatives analyses published by other governments or in the peer-reviewed literature), and setting clear timelines and more transparent work plans.
 Other Reforms aspects of California’s Safer Consumer Products program. Makes California’s program more efficient by granting data call-in authority to the program, streamlining processes (including giving the program the authority to regulate on the basis of alternatives analyses published by other governments or in the peer-reviewed literature), and setting clear timelines and more transparent work plans.
2022 Adopted SB 154
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Includes allocating $50,000,000 for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Water Appropriations bill. Includes allocating $50,000,000 for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
2022 Adopted AB 2771
Adopted
Restricts perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in cosmetic products. Effective January 1, 2025.
 Personal Care Products Restricts perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in cosmetic products. Effective January 1, 2025.
2022 Adopted AB 1817
Adopted
Restricts PFAS in apparel (both indoor and outdoor), accessories, and handbags starting in 2025. PFAS in outdoor apparel for severe wet conditions will be eliminated as of 2028 and manufacturers will be required to disclose the presence of PFAS in the products as of 2025. The law also directs manufacturers to use the least toxic alternative as they move away from PFAS.
 Textiles Restricts PFAS in apparel (both indoor and outdoor), accessories, and handbags starting in 2025. PFAS in outdoor apparel for severe wet conditions will be eliminated as of 2028 and manufacturers will be required to disclose the presence of PFAS in the products as of 2025. The law also directs manufacturers to use the least toxic alternative as they move away from PFAS.
2022 Adopted AB 180
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Includes $30 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
 Water Appropriations bill. Includes $30 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
2022 Adopted AB 178
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Includes $50 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
 Water Appropriations bill. Includes $50 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
2021 Adopted SB 343
Adopted
Requires the recyclability of a product or packaging to be truthful. Manufacturers would no longer be allowed to put the “chasing arrows” recycling symbol on items that aren’t actually recyclable. Products that contain PFAS chemicals can’t be labeled as recyclable.
 Plastics, Packaging Requires the recyclability of a product or packaging to be truthful. Manufacturers would no longer be allowed to put the “chasing arrows” recycling symbol on items that aren’t actually recyclable. Products that contain PFAS chemicals can’t be labeled as recyclable.
2021 Adopted SB 170
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Provides $30,000,000 for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address PFAS.
 Water Appropriations bill. Provides $30,000,000 for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address PFAS.
2021 Adopted AB 652
Adopted
Beginning July 1, 2023, bans the entire class of PFAS from a wide array of “juvenile” products including booster seats, changing pads, infant carriers, nursing pillows and crib mattresses.
 Children's Products Beginning July 1, 2023, bans the entire class of PFAS from a wide array of “juvenile” products including booster seats, changing pads, infant carriers, nursing pillows and crib mattresses.
2021 Adopted AB 1201
Adopted
Reforms labeling for compostable products, ensuring that an item that claims to be compostable actually is so. The law will also cut chemical contamination of compost by banning PFAS from any products labeled as compostable.
 Packaging Reforms labeling for compostable products, ensuring that an item that claims to be compostable actually is so. The law will also cut chemical contamination of compost by banning PFAS from any products labeled as compostable.
2021 Adopted AB 1200
Adopted
Beginning January 1, 2023, bans the use of PFAS chemicals in paper-based food packaging and, beginning January 1, 2024, requires disclosure of toxic substances in cookware, and ensures that a company isn’t misleading consumers by claiming that cookware is free of a particular toxin when it contains a similar compound in the same chemical class.
 Packaging Beginning January 1, 2023, bans the use of PFAS chemicals in paper-based food packaging and, beginning January 1, 2024, requires disclosure of toxic substances in cookware, and ensures that a company isn’t misleading consumers by claiming that cookware is free of a particular toxin when it contains a similar compound in the same chemical class.
2020 Adopted SB 312
Adopted
Requires cosmetics manufacturers to disclose lists of chemicals, flavors, and fragrances included in their products to the state along with associated health hazards. Requires the state to maintain a public website displaying the information.
 Personal Care Products Requires cosmetics manufacturers to disclose lists of chemicals, flavors, and fragrances included in their products to the state along with associated health hazards. Requires the state to maintain a public website displaying the information.
2020 Adopted SB 1371
Adopted
Allows state Water Board to require monitoring for and reporting of PFAS by water utilities.
 Water Allows state Water Board to require monitoring for and reporting of PFAS by water utilities.
2020 Adopted SB 1044
Adopted
Prohibits the manufacture and sale of firefighting foam containing PFAS, prohibits the use of PFAS foam for training purposes, requires manufacturers of firefighter protective equipment to disclose the inclusion of PFAS in their products. Effective January 1, 2022.
 Firefighter Gear, Firefighting Foam Prohibits the manufacture and sale of firefighting foam containing PFAS, prohibits the use of PFAS foam for training purposes, requires manufacturers of firefighter protective equipment to disclose the inclusion of PFAS in their products. Effective January 1, 2022.
2020 Adopted AB 2762
Adopted
Bans select chemicals in cosmetic products (specific phthalates, parabens, phenylenediamines, formaldehyde, mercury, and 13 PFAS).
 Personal Care Products Bans select chemicals in cosmetic products (specific phthalates, parabens, phenylenediamines, formaldehyde, mercury, and 13 PFAS).
2019 Adopted AB 756
Adopted
Requires public water systems to monitor for PFAS.
 Water Requires public water systems to monitor for PFAS.
2008 Adopted AB 1879
Adopted
Establishes a process to identify, prioritize and evaluate chemicals of concern in consumer products, determine how best to limit exposure or reduce the level of hazard, and establishes green chemistry challenge grants and a Green Ribbon Science Panel.
 Other Establishes a process to identify, prioritize and evaluate chemicals of concern in consumer products, determine how best to limit exposure or reduce the level of hazard, and establishes green chemistry challenge grants and a Green Ribbon Science Panel.

Colorado

6 adopted · 0 introduced · 6 total
Year Status Bill Issue Notes
2024 Adopted S.B.24-081
Adopted
Prohibits the sale or distribution of certain outdoor apparel intended for extreme or extended use in severe wet conditions that contain intentionally added PFAS chemicals. Prohibits PFAS in cleaning products, cookware, dental floss, menstruation products, ski wax, and textile articles. Prohibits a person from installing artificial turf that contains intentionally added PFAS chemicals on state property.
 Personal Care Products, Artificial Turf, Textiles Prohibits the sale or distribution of certain outdoor apparel intended for extreme or extended use in severe wet conditions that contain intentionally added PFAS chemicals. Prohibits PFAS in cleaning products, cookware, dental floss, menstruation products, ski wax, and textile articles. Prohibits a person from installing artificial turf that contains intentionally added PFAS chemicals on state property.
2022 Adopted HB 1348
Adopted
Establishes a regulatory scheme that requires disclosure of certain chemical information for products used in downhole oil and gas operations. The discloser must also provide the commission with a declaration that the chemical product contains no intentionally added perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl chemicals.
 Other Establishes a regulatory scheme that requires disclosure of certain chemical information for products used in downhole oil and gas operations. The discloser must also provide the commission with a declaration that the chemical product contains no intentionally added perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl chemicals.
2022 Adopted HB 1345
Adopted
Restricts the sale and distribution of consumer products containing PFAS chemicals. Consumer products include oil and gas products; carpets or rugs; cosmetics; fabric treatments; food packaging; juvenile products; textile furnishings; and upholstered furniture.
 Personal Care Products, Textiles, Packaging Restricts the sale and distribution of consumer products containing PFAS chemicals. Consumer products include oil and gas products; carpets or rugs; cosmetics; fabric treatments; food packaging; juvenile products; textile furnishings; and upholstered furniture.
2020 Adopted SB 20-218
Adopted
Creates fund PFAS cash fund, used to fund the PFAS grant program, the PFAS takeback program, and provide technical assistance in locating and studying PFAS to communities, stakeholders, and relevant regulatory bodies.
 Other Creates fund PFAS cash fund, used to fund the PFAS grant program, the PFAS takeback program, and provide technical assistance in locating and studying PFAS to communities, stakeholders, and relevant regulatory bodies.
2020 Adopted HB 20-1119
Adopted
Addresses when PFAS may be used for firefighting foam system testing both in general and in certain aircraft hangars. Requires the solid and hazardous waste commission to promulgate rules for both a certificate of registration for any facility, fire department, or lessee subject to federal rules and regulations that uses or stores PFAS in its operations and for standards for the capture and disposal of PFAS. Prohibits the use of class B firefighting foam that contains intentionally added PFAS in certain aircraft hangars beginning January 1, 2023.
 Firefighting Foam Addresses when PFAS may be used for firefighting foam system testing both in general and in certain aircraft hangars. Requires the solid and hazardous waste commission to promulgate rules for both a certificate of registration for any facility, fire department, or lessee subject to federal rules and regulations that uses or stores PFAS in its operations and for standards for the capture and disposal of PFAS. Prohibits the use of class B firefighting foam that contains intentionally added PFAS in certain aircraft hangars beginning January 1, 2023.
2019 Adopted HB 19-1279
Adopted
Prohibits the sale of PFAS-containing firefighting foam in certain circumstances, prohibits the use of PFAS foam for training, requires manufacturers to disclose if protective equipment they produce includes PFAS, and requires the Department of Health to conduct a survey to determine the amount of PFAS foam currently held, used, and disposed by fire departments.
 Firefighting Foam, Firefighter Gear Prohibits the sale of PFAS-containing firefighting foam in certain circumstances, prohibits the use of PFAS foam for training, requires manufacturers to disclose if protective equipment they produce includes PFAS, and requires the Department of Health to conduct a survey to determine the amount of PFAS foam currently held, used, and disposed by fire departments.

2026 Introduced S.B.17
Introduced
Allocates $1,500,000 to the Department of Emergency Services and Public Protection to support the removal of PFAS from fire apparatus.

2026 Introduced S.B.110
Introduced
Authorizes bonds to fund remediation of wells contaminated by PFAS in Franklin.
 Water Authorizes bonds to fund remediation of wells contaminated by PFAS in Franklin.
2026 Introduced H.B.5524
Introduced
Prohibits the use of polystyrene containers by restaurants and the provision of single-use food service items unless requested by a consumer. Prohibits the distribution of single-use straws that contain PFAS.
 Packaging, Plastics, Polystyrene Prohibits the use of polystyrene containers by restaurants and the provision of single-use food service items unless requested by a consumer. Prohibits the distribution of single-use straws that contain PFAS.
2026 Introduced H.B.5518
Introduced
Requires testing of PFAS substances in drinking water.
 Water Requires testing of PFAS substances in drinking water.
2024 Adopted S.B.292
Adopted
Prohibits the sale or distribution of the following products containing intentionally added PFAS: apparel, carpets or rugs, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, fabric treatments, children’s products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Requires manufacturers to submit information on products containing intentionally added PFAS to the state.
 Cleaning Products, Textiles, Personal Care Products, Children's Products Prohibits the sale or distribution of the following products containing intentionally added PFAS: apparel, carpets or rugs, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, fabric treatments, children’s products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Requires manufacturers to submit information on products containing intentionally added PFAS to the state.
2023 Adopted S.B.100
Adopted
Establishes an account in the general fund to provide grants to towns that need PFAS testing and remediation.
 Other Establishes an account in the general fund to provide grants to towns that need PFAS testing and remediation.
2021 Adopted SB 837
Adopted
Prohibits the use of firefighting foam containing PFAS and establishes a take-back program for such products. Effective October 1, 2021. The bill also bans intentionally added PFAS in food packaging no later than December 31, 2023.
 Packaging, Firefighting Foam Prohibits the use of firefighting foam containing PFAS and establishes a take-back program for such products. Effective October 1, 2021. The bill also bans intentionally added PFAS in food packaging no later than December 31, 2023.
2021 Adopted HB 6690
Adopted
Provides up to $1,150,000 in grants-in-aid to municipalities for the purpose of providing potable water and for assessment and remedial action to address PFAS pollution. Effective July 1, 2021.
 Water Provides up to $1,150,000 in grants-in-aid to municipalities for the purpose of providing potable water and for assessment and remedial action to address PFAS pollution. Effective July 1, 2021.
2021 Adopted HB 6666
Adopted
Requires water bottler companies to test the water source they are bottling for PFAS. Effective January 1, 2022.
 Water Requires water bottler companies to test the water source they are bottling for PFAS. Effective January 1, 2022.
2020 Adopted HB 5518
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Provides $2 million for PFAS cleanup and remediation.
 Other Appropriations bill. Provides $2 million for PFAS cleanup and remediation.

Year Status Bill Issue Notes
2026 Introduced S.B.538
Introduced

 Water Requires disclosure of PFAS usage by industrial users to publicly owned treatment works.
2026 Introduced H.B.1237
Introduced
Establishes producer responsibility regulations. Includes restrictions on certain addtives in packaging including lead, cadmium, mercury, PFAS, and phthalates.
 Packaging, Plastics, Packaging Reduction Establishes producer responsibility regulations. Includes restrictions on certain addtives in packaging including lead, cadmium, mercury, PFAS, and phthalates.
2026 Introduced H.B.1143
Introduced
Requires disclosure of all ingredients in menstrual products. Prohibits certain substances in menstrual products including lead, mercury, formaldehyde, triclosan, phthalates, and PFAS.
 Personal Care Products Requires disclosure of all ingredients in menstrual products. Prohibits certain substances in menstrual products including lead, mercury, formaldehyde, triclosan, phthalates, and PFAS.
2025 Introduced H.B.909
Introduced
Requires monitoring of PFAS levels at firefighter-related sites and prohibits the use of firefighting equipment containing PFAS as an intentionally added chemical after July 1, 2030. Establishes the Firefighter Health Advisory Council to monitor health risks and develop the Firefighter PFAS Initiative, which includes PFAS blood testing for firefighters.
 Firefighter Gear Requires monitoring of PFAS levels at firefighter-related sites and prohibits the use of firefighting equipment containing PFAS as an intentionally added chemical after July 1, 2030. Establishes the Firefighter Health Advisory Council to monitor health risks and develop the Firefighter PFAS Initiative, which includes PFAS blood testing for firefighters.
2025 Introduced H.B.611
Introduced
Requires publicly owned treatment works to collect information from industrial users about PFAS discharges.
 Water Requires publicly owned treatment works to collect information from industrial users about PFAS discharges.

2022 Adopted HB 1644
Adopted
Beginning December 31, 2024, prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of food packaging that contain perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Beginning July 1, 2024 prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of class B firefighting foam that contains intentionally added PFAS for training and testing purposes.
 Packaging, Firefighting Foam Beginning December 31, 2024, prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of food packaging that contain perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Prohibits the sale or distribution of certain products containing intentionally added PFAS including cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, menstrual products, intimate apparel, or food packaging or food contact products.

2025 Adopted H.B.2409
Adopted
Requires disclosure of PFAS in firefighting personal protective equipment (PPE). Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or distribution of firefighting PPE with intentionally added PFAS.

2025 Introduced H.B.1295
Introduced
Requires disclosure of products containing intentionally added PFAS. Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in certain products such as carpets, cookware, cosmetics, and food packaging.  Packaging, Personal Care Products, Textiles Requires disclosure of products containing intentionally added PFAS. Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in certain products such as carpets, cookware, cosmetics, and food packaging. Prohibits the sale of any product with intentionally added PFAS unless deemed currently unavoidable by the Pollution Control Board.
2023 Adopted H.B.3508
Adopted
Establishes a take-back program for fire departments that use and store firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. The program shall provide funding and resources to ensure the proper disposal or destruction of firefighting foam containing PFAS.
 Firefighting Foam, Disposal/Incineration Establishes a take-back program for fire departments that use and store firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. The program shall provide funding and resources to ensure the proper disposal or destruction of firefighting foam containing PFAS.
2022 Adopted HB 4818
Adopted
Prohibits the disposal by incineration of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances listed in the USEPA’s Toxic Release Inventory, including, but not limited to, aqueous film-forming foam.
 Disposal/Incineration Prohibits the disposal by incineration of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances listed in the USEPA’s Toxic Release Inventory, including, but not limited to, aqueous film-forming foam.

Prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of firefighting foam containing intentionally added PFAS. Effective January 1, 2025.

Introduced
Establishes a presumption of disability for firefighters diagnosed with health conditions related to PFAS.
 Other Establishes a presumption of disability for firefighters diagnosed with health conditions related to PFAS.
2026 Introduced H.B.1110
Introduced
Requires the department of environmental management to adopt maximum contaminant levels for PFAS chemicals and effluent limitation standards for wastewater containing PFAS chemicals by July 1, 2027. Establishes the PFAS chemical testing and remediation fund. Creates a PFAS chemical grant program.
 Water Requires the department of environmental management to adopt maximum contaminant levels for PFAS chemicals and effluent limitation standards for wastewater containing PFAS chemicals by July 1, 2027. Establishes the PFAS chemical testing and remediation fund. Creates a PFAS chemical grant program.

2025 Introduced H.F.588
Introduced
Prohibits the use, manufacture, distribution, and sale of consumer products containing PFAS substances including carpets/rgs, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental loss, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Prohibits the the sale of any product with intentionally added PFAS unless deemed an unavoidable use. Requires manufacturers to submit detailed information about products containing intentionally added PFAS.
 Cleaning Products, Textiles, Personal Care Products, Children's Products Prohibits the use, manufacture, distribution, and sale of consumer products containing PFAS substances including carpets/rgs, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental loss, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Requires manufacturers to submit detailed information about products containing intentionally added PFAS.

2025 Introduced L.D.1111
Introduced
Allocates $10 million to the Kennebec Water District to expand water infrastructure in Fairfield, Maine, addressing PFAS contamination in private wells.
 Water Allocates $10 million to the Kennebec Water District to expand water infrastructure in Fairfield, Maine, addressing PFAS contamination in private wells.
2025 Adopted L.D. 130
Adopted
Establishes PFAS Response Program to respond to and address PFAS contamination affecting agricultural producers.
 Other Establishes PFAS Response Program to respond to and address PFAS contamination affecting agricultural producers.
2023 Adopted L.D.1537
Adopted
Amends existing PFAS law. Beginning January 1, 2026, prohibits PFAS in cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, menstrual products, textile articles, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Beginning January 1, 2029, prohibits PFAS in artificial turf, outdoor apparel for extreme weather conditions, and certain refrigerants. Requires disclosure by manufacturers who seek an exemption from the ban on PFAS in all products beginning in 2032 unless they ask for a currently unavoidable use designation.
 Personal Care Products, Children's Products, Cleaning Products, Artificial Turf, Textiles Amends existing PFAS law. Beginning January 1, 2026, prohibits PFAS in cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, menstrual products, textile articles, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Beginning January 1, 2029, prohibits PFAS in artificial turf, outdoor apparel for extreme weather conditions, and certain refrigerants. Requires disclosure by manufacturers who seek an exemption from the ban on PFAS in all products beginning in 2032 unless they ask for a currently unavoidable use designation.
2023 Adopted L.D.1248
Adopted
Requires bottlers to conduct testing for PFAS. Prohibits the sale of bottled water that contains a regulated PFAS contaminant in an amount that exceeds the standard applicable to a community water system.
 Water Requires bottlers to conduct testing for PFAS. Prohibits the sale of bottled water that contains a regulated PFAS contaminant in an amount that exceeds the standard applicable to a community water system.
2023 Adopted L.D. 217
Adopted
Extends deadline for manufacturers to notify Maine DEP of their products for sale in the state that contain intentionally added PFAS from January 1, 2023 to January 1, 2025.
 Other Extends deadline for manufacturers to notify Maine DEP of their products for sale in the state that contain intentionally added PFAS from January 1, 2023 to January 1, 2025.
2023 Adopted L.D. 206
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Provides one-time funding to replace firefighting gear that is known to have perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Firefighter Gear Appropriations bill. Provides one-time funding to replace firefighting gear that is known to have perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
2023 Adopted L.D. 1591
Adopted
Promotes economic reuse of PFAS contaminated farmland through the Clean Energy Department.
 Other Promotes economic reuse of PFAS contaminated farmland through the Clean Energy Department.
2022 Adopted LD 2020
Adopted
Amends the law regulating firefighting and fire-suppressing foams to which PFAS have been intentionally added to provide that the prohibition on the manufacture, sale and distribution of such foams does not include foams manufactured, sold or distributed for a marine defense application if the use of the foam is required by the United States Department of Defense.
 Firefighting Foam Amends the law regulating firefighting and fire-suppressing foams to which PFAS have been intentionally added to provide that the prohibition on the manufacture, sale and distribution of such foams does not include foams manufactured, sold or distributed for a marine defense application if the use of the foam is required by the United States Department of Defense.
2022 Adopted LD 2019
Adopted
Beginning January 1, 2030, bans pesticides that contains intentionally added PFAS.
 Other Beginning January 1, 2030, bans pesticides that contains intentionally added PFAS.
2022 Adopted LD 1911
Adopted
Prohibits the spreading of PFAS-laden sludge and sludge-derived compost as fertilizer.
 Biosolids/Sludge Prohibits the spreading of PFAS-laden sludge and sludge-derived compost as fertilizer.
2022 Adopted LD 1875
Adopted
Requires state department to study methods of treating leachate from state-owned landfills to reduce the concentration of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in the leachate.
 Biosolids/Sludge Requires state department to study methods of treating leachate from state-owned landfills to reduce the concentration of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in the leachate.
2021 Adopted LD 558
Adopted
Directs the Department of Agriculture, Conservation, and Forestry to study alternative cropping systems for farmers affected by PFAS contamination.
 Other Directs the Department of Agriculture, Conservation, and Forestry to study alternative cropping systems for farmers affected by PFAS contamination.
2021 Adopted LD 363
Adopted
Provides that an action arising out of any harm or injury caused by a perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substance must be commenced within 6 years after the plaintiff discovers or should have discovered such harm or injury. It does not alter the 15 notice period or limitation period applicable to claims against a government entity.
 Other Provides that an action arising out of any harm or injury caused by a perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substance must be commenced within 6 years after the plaintiff discovers or should have discovered such harm or injury. It does not alter the 15 notice period or limitation period applicable to claims against a government entity.
2021 Adopted LD 264
Adopted
Directs the Board of Pesticides Control to gather information relating to perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in pesticides.
 Other Directs the Board of Pesticides Control to gather information relating to perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in pesticides.
2021 Adopted LD 221
Adopted
Appropriations bill. includes $10 million in funding to abate, clean up and mitigate threats or hazards posed by PFAS; $188,000 to fund a program to work directly with affected farmers on PFAS mitigation efforts; $3 million to establish and fund 17 positions to assist in the identification and management of PFAS; and $16.5 million to for PFAS testing, management, and treatment of drinking water.
 Other Appropriations bill. includes $10 million in funding to abate, clean up and mitigate threats or hazards posed by PFAS; $188,000 to fund a program to work directly with affected farmers on PFAS mitigation efforts; $3 million to establish and fund 17 positions to assist in the identification and management of PFAS; and $16.5 million to for PFAS testing, management, and treatment of drinking water.
2021 Adopted LD 1733
Adopted
Provides funding to expand efforts to address imminent risk to public health through investment in public water system improvements, including mitigation of lead in drinking water at schools and daycares and perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or PFAS, effects.
 Water Provides funding to expand efforts to address imminent risk to public health through investment in public water system improvements, including mitigation of lead in drinking water at schools and daycares and perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or PFAS, effects.
2021 Adopted LD 1600
Adopted
Creates the Land Application Contaminant Monitoring Fund to test and monitor soil and groundwater for PFAS substances and other contaminants. The fund is funded by a $10 per ton fee assessed on any disposal of septage, industrial sludge, municipal sludge, bioash, wood ash, or other residuals. Also requires the Department of Environmental Protection to test land that has received residuals for PFAS.
 Biosolids/Sludge Creates the Land Application Contaminant Monitoring Fund to test and monitor soil and groundwater for PFAS substances and other contaminants. The fund is funded by a $10 per ton fee assessed on any disposal of septage, industrial sludge, municipal sludge, bioash, wood ash, or other residuals. Also requires the Department of Environmental Protection to test land that has received residuals for PFAS.
2021 Adopted LD 1505
Adopted
Prohibits the use, manufacture, sale, and distribution of firefighting foam containing PFAS. Effective January 1, 2022.
 Firefighting Foam Prohibits the use, manufacture, sale, and distribution of firefighting foam containing PFAS. Effective January 1, 2022.
2021 Adopted LD 1503
Adopted
Requires manufacturers to report products containing intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2023. Prohibits the sale of residential carpets or rugs, and fabric treatments, that contain intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2023. Prohibits the sale of products containing intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2030 unless the use of PFAS in a product is specifically designated as “currently unavoidable”.
 Textiles, Packaging, Personal Care Products, Cleaning Products Requires manufacturers to report products containing intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2023. Prohibits the sale of residential carpets or rugs, and fabric treatments, that contain intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2023. Prohibits the sale of products containing intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2030 unless the use of PFAS in a product is specifically designated as “currently unavoidable”.
2021 Adopted LD 129
Adopted
Requires PFAS monitoring in drinking water and directs the Department of Health ad Human Services to establish maximum contaminant levels for PFAS substances by June 1, 2024.
 Water Requires PFAS monitoring in drinking water and directs the Department of Health ad Human Services to establish maximum contaminant levels for PFAS substances by June 1, 2024.
2019 Adopted LD 1433
Adopted
Prohibits the sale of food packaging with intentionally added toxic heavy metals, PFAS, or phthalates.
 Packaging Prohibits the sale of food packaging with intentionally added toxic heavy metals, PFAS, or phthalates.

Adopted
Selects up to seventy chemicals as Chemicals of High Concern based upon likely exposure to children or fetuses, and uses this list to designate Priority Chemicals which will require reporting and disclosure when used in children’s products.

 Cleaning Products, Building Materials/Construction, Personal Care Products, Packaging, Textiles Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or distribution of certain products containing intentionally added PFAS including cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, personal care products, feminine hygiene products, pet food packaging, juvenile products, intimacy products, fabric treatments, ski wax, textiles, textile furnishings, upholstered furniture, or paint.

Adopted
Requires the Department of the Environment to identify significant industrial users that currently and intentionally use PFAS chemicals by July 1, 2026; requires the Department to develop certain PFAS monitoring and testing criteria for certain significant industrial users by January 1, 2025; requires the Department by June 1, 2025, to develop PFAS action levels for addressing PFAS contamination from industrial discharge for pretreatment permits.
 Water Requires the Department of the Environment to identify significant industrial users that currently and intentionally use PFAS chemicals by July 1, 2026; requires the Department to develop certain PFAS monitoring and testing criteria for certain significant industrial users by January 1, 2025; requires the Department by June 1, 2025, to develop PFAS action levels for addressing PFAS contamination from industrial discharge for pretreatment permits.
2024 Adopted H.B.1147
Adopted
Prohibits playground surfacing materials that contain a certain concentration of lead or a component product, material, or substance to which PFAS chemicals were previously intentionally added for certain purposes.

2023 Adopted H.B.0848 / S.B.0407
Adopted

2023 Adopted H.F.2310
Adopted
Appropriates funding to address PFAS contamination. Restricts all unnecessary PFAS uses by 2032, bans 13 product categories specific uses, and requires companies to disclose presence of PFAS in any product. Prohibits the sale, distribution, and use of class B firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. Requires Pollution Control Agency to establish water quality standards for certain PFAS chemicals.
 Firefighting Foam, Water, Personal Care Products, Cleaning Products, Textiles Appropriates funding to address PFAS contamination. Restricts all unnecessary PFAS uses by 2032, bans 13 product categories specific uses, and requires companies to disclose presence of PFAS in any product. Prohibits the sale, distribution, and use of class B firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. Requires Pollution Control Agency to establish water quality standards for certain PFAS chemicals.
2023 Adopted H.F.100
Adopted
Packaging for cannabis flower, cannabinoid products, and hemp-derived consumer​ products must not contain or be coated with any perfluoroalkyl substance.

2022 Adopted HF 3765
Adopted
Appropriates funds to research and address PFAS contamination.
 Other Appropriates funds to research and address PFAS contamination.
2021 Adopted SF 20
Adopted
An omnibus bill that provides $600,000 in funding to develop and implement an initiative to reduce sources of PFAS upstream of wastewater treatment facilities; provides approximately $1.4 million in funding to develop strategies to manage PFAS in land-applied biosolids; provides $250,000 in funding to demonstrate new technology for protecting drinking water from PFAS; and prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution of food packaging containing intentionally added PFAS with an implementation date of Jan 1, 2024.
 Packaging An omnibus bill that provides $600,000 in funding to develop and implement an initiative to reduce sources of PFAS upstream of wastewater treatment facilities; provides approximately $1.4 million in funding to develop strategies to manage PFAS in land-applied biosolids; provides $250,000 in funding to demonstrate new technology for protecting drinking water from PFAS; and prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution of food packaging containing intentionally added PFAS with an implementation date of Jan 1, 2024.

Adopted
Bans manufacture and sale of halogenated, phosphorus-based, nitrogen-based, and nanoscale flame retardants in residential upholstered furniture, children’s products, and residential and business textiles. Prohibits the manufacture and sale of PFAS-containing firefighting foam.
 Firefighting Foam, Textiles, Children's Products Bans manufacture and sale of halogenated, phosphorus-based, nitrogen-based, and nanoscale flame retardants in residential upholstered furniture, children’s products, and residential and business textiles. Prohibits the manufacture and sale of PFAS-containing firefighting foam.

Missouri

Year Status Bill Issue Notes
2026 Introduced H.B.2400
Introduced
Prohibits the sale or distribution of products containing intentionally added PFAS including carpets, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Nevada

1 adopted · 0 introduced · 1 total
Year Status Bill Issue Notes
2021 Adopted AB 97
Adopted
Prohibits the use of firefighting foam containing PFAS; establishes standards for the disposal of firefighting foam containing PFAS; establishes a working group to study issues related to PFAS and develop recommendations for state action; prohibits any flame retardant organohalogenated chemical in the manufacture, sale, and distribution of children’s products, upholstered residential furniture, residential textiles, business textiles, or mattresses (specifically exempts polymeric flame retardants).
 Children's Products, Firefighting Foam, Textiles Prohibits the use of firefighting foam containing PFAS; establishes standards for the disposal of firefighting foam containing PFAS; establishes a working group to study issues related to PFAS and develop recommendations for state action; prohibits any flame retardant organohalogenated chemical in the manufacture, sale, and distribution of children’s products, upholstered residential furniture, residential textiles, business textiles, or mattresses (specifically exempts polymeric flame retardants).

Directs the department of environmental services and the department of health and human services to enter into a memorandum of understanding on cooperation regarding data sharing. Requires Oregon Health Authority to adopt and maintain list of designated high priority chemicals of concern used in cosmetic products and to periodically review and revise list. Requires manufacturers of cosmetic products sold in state to include on manufacturer’s website notice of certain chemicals used in products, beginning on January 1, 2027. Bans manufacture, sale and distribution of cosmetic products containing certain chemicals and classes of chemicals in state, beginning on January 1, 2027.
 Personal Care Products Requires Oregon Health Authority to adopt and maintain list of designated high priority chemicals of concern used in cosmetic products and to periodically review and revise list. Requires manufacturers of cosmetic products sold in state to include on manufacturer’s website notice of certain chemicals used in products, beginning on January 1, 2027. Bans manufacture, sale and distribution of cosmetic products containing certain chemicals and classes of chemicals in state, beginning on January 1, 2027.
2023 Adopted S.B.543
Adopted
Prohibits food vendor from using polystyrene foam container in sales of prepared food. Prohibits person from selling or distributing foodware containers containing perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Polystyrene, Plastics, Packaging Prohibits food vendor from using polystyrene foam container in sales of prepared food. Prohibits person from selling or distributing foodware containers containing perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substances.
2015 Adopted SB 478
Adopted
The Oregon Toxic Free Kids Act (TFKA) establishes a list of high priority chemicals of concern for children’s products, posts the list online, requires manufacturer disclosure of these chemicals, and requires their eventual removal. Authorizes participation in the Interstate Chemicals Clearinghouse. Please note: The Oregon Health Authority (OHA) is in its final stages of implementing TFKA. The current final phase of rulemaking focuses on the exemptions manufacturers may seek if they are unable to completely phase out these toxic chemicals. OHA must finalize these rules in order for TFKA to be fully enforceable. Once that happens, OEC will continue watchdogging the law to ensure that manufacturers are abiding by its requirements.
 Children's Products The Oregon Toxic Free Kids Act (TFKA) establishes a list of high priority chemicals of concern for children’s products, posts the list online, requires manufacturer disclosure of these chemicals, and requires their eventual removal. Authorizes participation in the Interstate Chemicals Clearinghouse. Please note: The Oregon Health Authority (OHA) is in its final stages of implementing TFKA. The current final phase of rulemaking focuses on the exemptions manufacturers may seek if they are unable to completely phase out these toxic chemicals. OHA must finalize these rules in order for TFKA to be fully enforceable. Once that happens, OEC will continue watchdogging the law to ensure that manufacturers are abiding by its requirements.

2024 Adopted H.B.7619
Adopted
Updates the existing Toxic Packaging Act by delaying the ban on PFAS in food packaging until January 1, 2025, and in processing agents until July 1, 2027. Includes a prohibition on lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, and PFAS in packaging and packaging components.
 Packaging Updates the existing Toxic Packaging Act by delaying the ban on PFAS in food packaging until January 1, 2025, and in processing agents until July 1, 2027. Includes a prohibition on lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, and PFAS in packaging and packaging components.

2021 Adopted S 20
Adopted
Prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of firefighting foam containing intentionally added PFAS beginning October 1, 2023 . Requires manufacturers selling firefighting equipment to disclose if it contains PFAS. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution of food packaging containing intentionally added PFAS, inks, dyes, pigments, adhesives, stabilizers, coatings, plasticizers, or any other additives to which ortho-phthalates have been intentionally added. Allows the Department of Health to adopt rules prohibiting bisphenols in food packaging. Prohibits PFAS in carpets, rugs, fabric treatments, and ski wax.
 Textiles, Firefighting Foam, Packaging Prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of firefighting foam containing intentionally added PFAS beginning October 1, 2023 . Requires manufacturers selling firefighting equipment to disclose if it contains PFAS. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution of food packaging containing intentionally added PFAS, inks, dyes, pigments, adhesives, stabilizers, coatings, plasticizers, or any other additives to which ortho-phthalates have been intentionally added. Allows the Department of Health to adopt rules prohibiting bisphenols in food packaging. Prohibits PFAS in carpets, rugs, fabric treatments, and ski wax.
2020 Adopted H 955
Adopted
Appropriates $550k to improve public water systems contaminated with PFAS, and $50k to reimburse schools with contaminated water who must utilize alternate water supplies.
 Water Appropriates $550k to improve public water systems contaminated with PFAS, and $50k to reimburse schools with contaminated water who must utilize alternate water supplies.
2019 Adopted S 49
Adopted
Sets Maximum Contaminant Levels for 5 PFAS chemicals in water of 20ppt each and cumulatively, requires testing for PFAS chemicals, and requires landfills to treat leachate to remove PFAS chemicals.
 Water Sets Maximum Contaminant Levels for 5 PFAS chemicals in water of 20ppt each and cumulatively, requires testing for PFAS chemicals, and requires landfills to treat leachate to remove PFAS chemicals.
2017 Adopted S10
Adopted
Creates liability and penalties for contaminating water supplies with perfluorinated chemicals.
 Water Creates liability and penalties for contaminating water supplies with perfluorinated chemicals.
2014 Adopted S 239
Adopted

Adopted
Authorizes a locality to require testing and monitoring for PFAS when sewage sludge is land applied within its jurisdiction.
 Biosolids/Sludge Authorizes a locality to require testing and monitoring for PFAS when sewage sludge is land applied within its jurisdiction.
2025 Adopted H.B.2050
Adopted
Requires monitoring and reduction of PFAS levels in the Occoquan Reservoir by facilities discharging industrial wastewater.
 Water Requires monitoring and reduction of PFAS levels in the Occoquan Reservoir by facilities discharging industrial wastewater.
2024 Adopted S.B.243 / H.B.1085
Adopted
Requires the owner or operator of a publicly owned treatment works to monitor PFAS levels in effluent, influent, and biosolids at least quarterly and report all such monitoring data. Requires the Department of Environmental Quality to develop a PFAS action plan to identify and address sources of certain PFAS. Requires certain facilities that manufacture or use PFAS to report the use of such chemicals to the Department and to monitor such PFAS.
 Water, Biosolids/Sludge Requires the owner or operator of a publicly owned treatment works to monitor PFAS levels in effluent, influent, and biosolids at least quarterly and report all such monitoring data. Requires the Department of Environmental Quality to develop a PFAS action plan to identify and address sources of certain PFAS. Requires certain facilities that manufacture or use PFAS to report the use of such chemicals to the Department and to monitor such PFAS.
2023 Adopted H.B. 2189
Adopted
Requires industrial users of publicly owned treatment works that receive and clean, repair, refurbish, or process items that contain PFAS to test wastestreams for PFAS prior to and after cleaning, repairing, refurbishing, or processing such items.
 Water Requires industrial users of publicly owned treatment works that receive and clean, repair, refurbish, or process items that contain PFAS to test wastestreams for PFAS prior to and after cleaning, repairing, refurbishing, or processing such items.
2022 Adopted HB 919
Adopted
Requires the Board of Health to adopt maximum contaminant levels in all water supplies and waterworks in the Commonwealth for perfluorooctanoic acid, perfluorooctane sulfonate, and for such other perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Water Requires the Board of Health to adopt maximum contaminant levels in all water supplies and waterworks in the Commonwealth for perfluorooctanoic acid, perfluorooctane sulfonate, and for such other perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.

Adopted
Tasks Commissioner of Health to convene work group to study occurrence of PFAS in state drinking water and develop recommendations for Maximum Contaminant Levels.

Adopted
Beginning January 1, 2025, no person may manufacture, knowingly sell, offer for sale, distribute for sale, or distribute for use in this state any cosmetic product that contains any of the following intentionally added chemicals or chemical classes: ortho-phthalates, perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances, formaldehyde, mercury, triclosan, and more.

2021 Adopted HB 1080
Adopted
A 2021-2022 appropriations bill that includes providing $5,950,000 to the City of DuPont for PFAS treatment, $5,569,000 to Lakewood Water District for a PFAS Treatment Facility, and $1,150,00 for a PFAS Pilot Project by the Department of Ecology.

2020 Adopted HB 2265
Adopted
Eliminates exemptions from restrictions on use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam.
 Firefighting Foam Eliminates exemptions from restrictions on use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam.
2019 Adopted SB 5135
Adopted
Directs the Department of Ecology to identify and take regulatory action on consumer products that are a significant source of chemicals that are a concern for sensitive populations and species. Prioritizes PCBs, PFAS, organohalogen flame retardants, phthalates, and phenolic compounds (BPA, APEs) for initial consideration.
 Other Directs the Department of Ecology to identify and take regulatory action on consumer products that are a significant source of chemicals that are a concern for sensitive populations and species. Prioritizes PCBs, PFAS, organohalogen flame retardants, phthalates, and phenolic compounds (BPA, APEs) for initial consideration.
2018 Adopted SB 6413
Adopted
Prohibits the manufacture and sale of class B firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. Requires manufacturers of firefighting personal protective equipment (PPE) to notify buyers in Washington if their products contain PFAS.

Introduced
Imposes a 50% gross revenues tax on PFAS product manufacturers and retailers in Minnesota, establishes a cleanup fund for environmental purposes, and creates an advisory commission to oversee fund allocation.

Introduced
Imposes a tax on PFAS product manufacturers and retailers in Minnesota, establishes a cleanup fund for environmental purposes, and creates an advisory commission to oversee fund usage.

Introduced
Creates the agricultural PFAS remediation fund in order to assist farmers in New Hampshire. Imposes a 5 year moratorium on the spreading and application of sludge and biosolids for agricultural use.

Introduced
Prohibits the sale of medical adhesives and bandages containing PFAS.
 Other Prohibits the sale of medical adhesives and bandages containing PFAS.
New York Introduced S.672A
Introduced
Amends New York’s environmental conservation law to fund restoration projects, redefines the definition of “contaminant” to include hazardous waste, petroleum, certain chemicals, and PFAS substances.
 Other Amends New York’s environmental conservation law to fund restoration projects, redefines the definition of “contaminant” to include hazardous waste, petroleum, certain chemicals, and PFAS substances.

Introduced
Establishes a moratorium on biosolid land application, mandates PFAS testing, creates a response program and fund for affected farms, and forms a task force to evaluate PFAS risks and disposal methods.

New York Introduced S.461
Introduced
Prohibits the use of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in industrial plants, which are commonly found in items such as vats, conveyor belts, molds, ovens, pans, pots, and chemicals used as mold releasers, lubricants, seals, and coatings.

Introduced
Require companies selling or distributing packaging materials and products to register with a packaging reduction organization to develop a packaging reduction and recycling plan. Adopted
Requires the Department of the Environment to identify significant industrial users that currently and intentionally use PFAS chemicals by July 1, 2026; requires the Department to develop certain PFAS monitoring and testing criteria for certain significant industrial users by January 1, 2025; requires the Department by June 1, 2025, to develop PFAS action levels for addressing PFAS contamination from industrial discharge for pretreatment permits.

Allocates for regional drinking water infrastructure as part of Phase 2 of the southern New Hampshire regional water project to increase water supply by over 2 million gallons per day to multiple towns impacted by per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance contamination and growing water demands.
 Water Appropriations bill. Allocates for regional drinking water infrastructure as part of Phase 2 of the southern New Hampshire regional water project to increase water supply by over 2 million gallons per day to multiple towns impacted by per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance contamination and growing water demands.
New Hampshire Adopted H.B.1352
Adopted
Requires disclosure of firefighting personal protective equipment containing PFAS chemicals. Encourages the prioritization of PFAS-free equipment purchases. Beginning January 1, 2025, prohibits the provision of equipment containing intentionally added PFAS.
 Firefighter Gear Requires disclosure of firefighting personal protective equipment containing PFAS chemicals. Encourages the prioritization of PFAS-free equipment purchases. Beginning January 1, 2025, prohibits the provision of equipment containing intentionally added PFAS.
New Hampshire Adopted H.B.1114
Adopted
Extends the duration of a commission investigating the environmental and public health impacts of perfluorinated chemical releases in Merrimack, Bedford, Londonderry, Hudson, and Litchfield, New Hampshire, for an additional five years.
 Water Extends the duration of a commission investigating the environmental and public health impacts of perfluorinated chemical releases in Merrimack, Bedford, Londonderry, Hudson, and Litchfield, New Hampshire, for an additional five years.
New Jersey Adopted A.4760 / S.3176
Adopted
Requires the Department of Environmental Protection and the Drinking Water Quality Institute to perform a study concerning the regulation and treatment of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Water Requires the Department of Environmental Protection and the Drinking Water Quality Institute to perform a study concerning the regulation and treatment of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
New Jersey Adopted A.4125
Adopted
Prohibits sale, manufacture, distribution, and use of firefighting foam containing intentionally added perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Requires DEP to establish a collection and disposal program.
 Firefighting Foam Prohibits sale, manufacture, distribution, and use of firefighting foam containing intentionally added perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Requires DEP to establish a collection and disposal program.
New York Adopted A.8804A
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Includes up to $500,000 to the State University of New York at Stony Brook to provide for a new laboratory testing facility for PFOA and other chemicals.
 Other Appropriations bill. Includes up to $500,000 to the State University of New York at Stony Brook to provide for a new laboratory testing facility for PFOA and other chemicals.
Rhode Island Adopted S.B.2152
Adopted
Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in certain products including artificial turf, carpets or rugs, cookware, cosmetics, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstrual products, ski wax, and textile articles. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or use of firefighting foam containing PFAS. Requires disclosure of PFAS in firefighting personal protective equipment.
 Artificial Turf, Firefighting Foam, Children's Products, Personal Care Products, Textiles Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in certain products including artificial turf, carpets or rugs, cookware, cosmetics, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstrual products, ski wax, and textile articles. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or use of firefighting foam containing PFAS. Requires disclosure of PFAS in firefighting personal protective equipment.
Rhode Island Adopted H.B.7619
Adopted
Updates the existing Toxic Packaging Act by delaying the ban on PFAS in food packaging until January 1, 2025, and in processing agents until July 1, 2027. Includes a prohibition on lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, and PFAS in packaging and packaging components.
 Packaging Updates the existing Toxic Packaging Act by delaying the ban on PFAS in food packaging until January 1, 2025, and in processing agents until July 1, 2027. Includes a prohibition on lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, and PFAS in packaging and packaging components.
Vermont Adopted S.25
Adopted
Prohibit the manufacture, sale, and distribution of cosmetic and menstrual products containing certain chemicals and chemical classes. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution in Vermont of textiles containing perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Prohibits the installationof any new athletic turf field containing perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Artificial Turf, Personal Care Products, Textiles Prohibit the manufacture, sale, and distribution of cosmetic and menstrual products containing certain chemicals and chemical classes. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution in Vermont of textiles containing perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Prohibits the installationof any new athletic turf field containing perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
Virginia Adopted S.B.243 / H.B.1085
Adopted
Requires the owner or operator of a publicly owned treatment works to monitor PFAS levels in effluent, influent, and biosolids at least quarterly and report all such monitoring data. Requires the Department of Environmental Quality to develop a PFAS action plan to identify and address sources of certain PFAS. Requires certain facilities that manufacture or use PFAS to report the use of such chemicals to the Department and to monitor such PFAS.
 Water, Biosolids/Sludge Requires the owner or operator of a publicly owned treatment works to monitor PFAS levels in effluent, influent, and biosolids at least quarterly and report all such monitoring data. Requires the Department of Environmental Quality to develop a PFAS action plan to identify and address sources of certain PFAS. Requires certain facilities that manufacture or use PFAS to report the use of such chemicals to the Department and to monitor such PFAS.

Appropriations bill. Provides $5 million for PFAS mitigation.

Appropriations bill. Provides $90 million for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.

Connecticut Adopted S.B.100
Adopted
Establishes an account in the general fund to provide grants to towns that need PFAS testing and remediation.
 Other Establishes an account in the general fund to provide grants to towns that need PFAS testing and remediation.
Illinois Adopted H.B.3508
Adopted
Establishes a take-back program for fire departments that use and store firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. The program shall provide funding and resources to ensure the proper disposal or destruction of firefighting foam containing PFAS.
 Firefighting Foam, Disposal/Incineration Establishes a take-back program for fire departments that use and store firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. The program shall provide funding and resources to ensure the proper disposal or destruction of firefighting foam containing PFAS.
Indiana Adopted H.B.1341
Adopted
Beginning June 30, 2024, prohibits fire departments from purchasing firefighter gear unless it contains a label indicating whether or not the firefighter gear contains a PFAS chemical.
 Firefighter Gear Beginning June 30, 2024, prohibits fire departments from purchasing firefighter gear unless it contains a label indicating whether or not the firefighter gear contains a PFAS chemical.
Indiana Adopted H.B.1219
Adopted
Establishes a PFAS biomonitoring pilot program under the department of homeland security to collect and analyze blood samples of individuals who were previously, or are currently, firefighters.
 Other Establishes a PFAS biomonitoring pilot program under the department of homeland security to collect and analyze blood samples of individuals who were previously, or are currently, firefighters.
Maine Adopted L.D.1537
Adopted
Amends existing PFAS law. Beginning January 1, 2026, prohibits PFAS in cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, menstrual products, textile articles, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Beginning January 1, 2029, prohibits PFAS in artificial turf, outdoor apparel for extreme weather conditions, and certain refrigerants. Requires disclosure by manufacturers who seek an exemption from the ban on PFAS in all products beginning in 2032 unless they ask for a currently unavoidable use designation.
 Personal Care Products, Children's Products, Cleaning Products, Artificial Turf, Textiles Amends existing PFAS law. Beginning January 1, 2026, prohibits PFAS in cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, menstrual products, textile articles, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Beginning January 1, 2029, prohibits PFAS in artificial turf, outdoor apparel for extreme weather conditions, and certain refrigerants. Requires disclosure by manufacturers who seek an exemption from the ban on PFAS in all products beginning in 2032 unless they ask for a currently unavoidable use designation.
Maine Adopted L.D.1248
Adopted
Requires bottlers to conduct testing for PFAS. Prohibits the sale of bottled water that contains a regulated PFAS contaminant in an amount that exceeds the standard applicable to a community water system.
 Water Requires bottlers to conduct testing for PFAS. Prohibits the sale of bottled water that contains a regulated PFAS contaminant in an amount that exceeds the standard applicable to a community water system.
Maine Adopted L.D. 217
Adopted
Extends deadline for manufacturers to notify Maine DEP of their products for sale in the state that contain intentionally added PFAS from January 1, 2023 to January 1, 2025.
 Other Extends deadline for manufacturers to notify Maine DEP of their products for sale in the state that contain intentionally added PFAS from January 1, 2023 to January 1, 2025.
Maine Adopted L.D. 206
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Provides one-time funding to replace firefighting gear that is known to have perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Firefighter Gear Appropriations bill. Provides one-time funding to replace firefighting gear that is known to have perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
Maine Adopted L.D. 1591
Adopted
Promotes economic reuse of PFAS contaminated farmland through the Clean Energy Department.
 Other Promotes economic reuse of PFAS contaminated farmland through the Clean Energy Department.
Maryland Adopted H.B.0848 / S.B.0407
Adopted
Establishes the Innovative Potable Reuse Pilot Program in the Department of the Environment for the purpose of authorizing the regulated use of reclaimed water as a source for certain drinking water facilities. The reclaimed water must meet maximum concentrations of PFAS chemicals established by the department.
 Water Establishes the Innovative Potable Reuse Pilot Program in the Department of the Environment for the purpose of authorizing the regulated use of reclaimed water as a source for certain drinking water facilities. The reclaimed water must meet maximum concentrations of PFAS chemicals established by the department.
Maryland Adopted H.B.0319 / S.B.0158
Adopted
Requires the Department of Agriculture to study the use of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in pesticides and requires the Department to report its findings and recommendations on or before November 1, 2023.
 Other Requires the Department of Agriculture to study the use of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in pesticides and requires the Department to report its findings and recommendations on or before November 1, 2023.
Michigan Adopted H.B.4437
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Provides $39 million to address PFAS and emerging contaminants.
 Other Appropriations bill. Provides $39 million to address PFAS and emerging contaminants.
Minnesota Adopted H.F.2310
Adopted
Appropriates funding to address PFAS contamination. Restricts all unnecessary PFAS uses by 2032, bans 13 product categories specific uses, and requires companies to disclose presence of PFAS in any product. Prohibits the sale, distribution, and use of class B firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. Requires Pollution Control Agency to establish water quality standards for certain PFAS chemicals.
 Firefighting Foam, Water, Personal Care Products, Cleaning Products, Textiles Appropriates funding to address PFAS contamination. Restricts all unnecessary PFAS uses by 2032, bans 13 product categories specific uses, and requires companies to disclose presence of PFAS in any product. Prohibits the sale, distribution, and use of class B firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. Requires Pollution Control Agency to establish water quality standards for certain PFAS chemicals.
Minnesota Adopted H.F.100
Adopted
Packaging for cannabis flower, cannabinoid products, and hemp-derived consumer​ products must not contain or be coated with any perfluoroalkyl substance.
 Packaging Packaging for cannabis flower, cannabinoid products, and hemp-derived consumer​ products must not contain or be coated with any perfluoroalkyl substance.
New Hampshire Adopted H.B.391
Adopted
Directs the department of environmental services and the department of health and human services to enter into a memorandum of understanding on cooperation regarding data sharing. Changes the reporting requirement from 6 months to an annual basis and requires it to be submitted to the commission on the environmental and public health impacts of PFAS.
 Other Directs the department of environmental services and the department of health and human services to enter into a memorandum of understanding on cooperation regarding data sharing. Changes the reporting requirement from 6 months to an annual basis and requires it to be submitted to the commission on the environmental and public health impacts of PFAS.
Oregon Adopted S.B.546
Adopted
Requires Oregon Health Authority to adopt and maintain list of designated high priority chemicals of concern used in cosmetic products and to periodically review and revise list. Requires manufacturers of cosmetic products sold in state to include on manufacturer’s website notice of certain chemicals used in products, beginning on January 1, 2027. Bans manufacture, sale and distribution of cosmetic products containing certain chemicals and classes of chemicals in state, beginning on January 1, 2027.
 Personal Care Products Requires Oregon Health Authority to adopt and maintain list of designated high priority chemicals of concern used in cosmetic products and to periodically review and revise list. Requires manufacturers of cosmetic products sold in state to include on manufacturer’s website notice of certain chemicals used in products, beginning on January 1, 2027. Bans manufacture, sale and distribution of cosmetic products containing certain chemicals and classes of chemicals in state, beginning on January 1, 2027.
Oregon Adopted S.B.543
Adopted
Prohibits food vendor from using polystyrene foam container in sales of prepared food. Prohibits person from selling or distributing foodware containers containing perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substances.
 Polystyrene, Plastics, Packaging Prohibits food vendor from using polystyrene foam container in sales of prepared food. Prohibits person from selling or distributing foodware containers containing perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substances.
Rhode Island Adopted H.B.5861 / S.B.724
Adopted
Provides that water supply systems that have PFAS contaminants that exceed the interim drinking water standard be required to enter into a consent agreement with department of health to implement treatment to reduce the levels of PFAS contaminants.
 Water Provides that water supply systems that have PFAS contaminants that exceed the interim drinking water standard be required to enter into a consent agreement with department of health to implement treatment to reduce the levels of PFAS contaminants.
Vermont Adopted S.73
Adopted
Requires the Director of the Division of Fire Safety to submit a report on the projected cost for the state to fund cancer screenings for all career and volunteer firefighters and replace all personal protective equipment.

Adopted
Appropriations bill. Provides $3 million to the Department of Environmental Conservation for PFAS remediation.

Virginia Adopted H.B. 2189
Adopted
Requires industrial users of publicly owned treatment works that receive and clean, repair, refurbish, or process items that contain PFAS to test wastestreams for PFAS prior to and after cleaning, repairing, refurbishing, or processing such items.
 Water Requires industrial users of publicly owned treatment works that receive and clean, repair, refurbish, or process items that contain PFAS to test wastestreams for PFAS prior to and after cleaning, repairing, refurbishing, or processing such items.
Washington Adopted H.B. 1047
Adopted
Beginning January 1, 2025, no person may manufacture, knowingly sell, offer for sale, distribute for sale, or distribute for use in this state any cosmetic product that contains any of the following intentionally added chemicals or chemical classes: ortho-phthalates, perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances, formaldehyde, mercury, triclosan, and more.
 Personal Care Products Beginning January 1, 2025, no person may manufacture, knowingly sell, offer for sale, distribute for sale, or distribute for use in this state any cosmetic product that contains any of the following intentionally added chemicals or chemical classes: ortho-phthalates, perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances, formaldehyde, mercury, triclosan, and more.
Wisconsin Adopted S.B.70
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Transfers $110 million from the general fund to the PFAS fund. Transfers $15 million from the environmental management fund to the PFAS fund.
 Water Appropriations bill. Transfers $110 million from the general fund to the PFAS fund. Transfers $15 million from the environmental management fund to the PFAS fund.
2022 32 bills
State Status Bill Issue Notes
California Adopted SB 502
Adopted
Reforms aspects of California’s Safer Consumer Products program. Makes California’s program more efficient by granting data call-in authority to the program, streamlining processes (including giving the program the authority to regulate on the basis of alternatives analyses published by other governments or in the peer-reviewed literature), and setting clear timelines and more transparent work plans.
 Other Reforms aspects of California’s Safer Consumer Products program. Makes California’s program more efficient by granting data call-in authority to the program, streamlining processes (including giving the program the authority to regulate on the basis of alternatives analyses published by other governments or in the peer-reviewed literature), and setting clear timelines and more transparent work plans.

Adopted
Appropriations bill. Includes allocating $50,000,000 for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.

California Adopted AB 2771
Adopted
Restricts perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in cosmetic products. Effective January 1, 2025.
 Personal Care Products Restricts perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in cosmetic products. Effective January 1, 2025.
California Adopted AB 1817
Adopted
Restricts PFAS in apparel (both indoor and outdoor), accessories, and handbags starting in 2025. PFAS in outdoor apparel for severe wet conditions will be eliminated as of 2028 and manufacturers will be required to disclose the presence of PFAS in the products as of 2025. The law also directs manufacturers to use the least toxic alternative as they move away from PFAS.
 Textiles Restricts PFAS in apparel (both indoor and outdoor), accessories, and handbags starting in 2025. PFAS in outdoor apparel for severe wet conditions will be eliminated as of 2028 and manufacturers will be required to disclose the presence of PFAS in the products as of 2025. The law also directs manufacturers to use the least toxic alternative as they move away from PFAS.
California Adopted AB 180
Adopted
Appropriations bill. Includes $30 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
 Water Appropriations bill. Includes $30 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
California Adopted AB 178

Appropriations bill. Includes $50 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
 Water Appropriations bill. Includes $50 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
Colorado Adopted HB 1348
Adopted
Establishes a regulatory scheme that requires disclosure of certain chemical information for products used in downhole oil and gas operations. The discloser must also provide the commission with a declaration that the chemical product contains no intentionally added perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl chemicals.
 Other Establishes a regulatory scheme that requires disclosure of certain chemical information for products used in downhole oil and gas operations. The discloser must also provide the commission with a declaration that the chemical product contains no intentionally added perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl chemicals.
Colorado Adopted HB 1345
Adopted
Restricts the sale and distribution of consumer products containing PFAS chemicals. Consumer products include oil and gas products; carpets or rugs; cosmetics; fabric treatments; food packaging; juvenile products; textile furnishings; and upholstered furniture.
 Personal Care Products, Textiles, Packaging Restricts the sale and distribution of consumer products containing PFAS chemicals. Consumer products include oil and gas products; carpets or rugs; cosmetics; fabric treatments; food packaging; juvenile products; textile furnishings; and upholstered furniture.
